_note: How to price X as an electrician — trade-side pricing guides. Audience: UK self-employed electricians, sub-contractors and small business owners deciding what to charge. NOT homeowner cost pages. Updated 2026-05-18.
_policy: Always electrician-side. Frame as 'what you should charge for fair margin and survival', not 'what's cheap'. Justify rates with insurance, scheme fees, calibrated kit, liability, A4:2026 compliance overhead.

jobs.name: EICR (Periodic Inspection)
jobs.audience: self-employed electricians and small contractors quoting EICRs
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Chapter 65 + Schedule of Inspections, IET GN3 9th Edition, Electrical Safety Standards in the PRS Regs 2020
keyConsiderations: Charge based on number of circuits + property complexity, not pure hours — older properties with no documentation take longer than 5-circuit new-build flats
Always quote inspection + remedial separately — never include unknown remedial in the EICR fee
Factor in your scheme fees (~£500/year NICEIC or NAPIT), professional indemnity (~£500-1000/year), calibrated MFT (~£1,200 every 3-5 years)
PRS-deadline jobs (5-year cycle approaching) command premium pricing — landlords need it done in time
Code C1 and C2 observations require you to recommend remedial — the EICR fee covers the report, not the fix
commonMistakes: Pricing below scheme members — you'll be undercut by uninsured electricians and end up doing risk-laden work for a loss
Including 'minor remedial' in the EICR price — quote remedial separately, every time
Not charging for the certificate write-up time — typical EICR is 60-80% on-site, 20-40% paperwork
Forgetting commercial premises charge per socket/fitting, not per hour

jobs.name: Consumer Unit Replacement
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Chapter 53 + Section 442 + AFDD Reg 421.1.7.101), Part P notification, BS EN 61439-3
keyConsiderations: Price the FULL spec including SPD, AFDDs (where mandatory), main switch, RCBOs, labels, EIC certification — not the cheapest possible config
Tails upgrade is a separate price band — coordinate with the DNO if needed (£80-180 add-on typical)
Bonding upgrades (gas, water, oil) are frequently needed — quote conditionally on inspection
Three-phase boards command 1.5-2x domestic prices because of equipment cost + complexity
Travel + setup time is often skipped from quotes — include it as a minimum visit fee
commonMistakes: Quoting a 'CU swap' without specifying RCBO type (AC vs A) — Type A is now standard under A4:2026 considerations
Forgetting SPD — Chapter 443 makes SPDs effectively mandatory in most installations
Skipping AFDD on jobs where they're required (HMOs, care homes) — opens you to liability
Including remedial bonding in the fixed price — quote conditionally

jobs.name: EV Charger Installation
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Section 722, IET Code of Practice for EV Charging Equipment Installation (5th Edition), Electric Vehicles (Smart Charge Points) Regulations 2021
keyConsiderations: OZEV-approved installer status earns a premium — homeowners need it for the grant
Cable run distance dominates pricing — every metre over 15m adds material cost AND labour
Type B RCD (or RDC-DD device) is mandatory — not optional, not negotiable on price
DNO notification (G98/G99) takes time — bake into your fee
Section 722 + A4:2026 changes mean ongoing kit upgrades — price for current compliance, not legacy
V2G / V2H bidirectional adds significant complexity — premium pricing justified
commonMistakes: Pricing as 'just another circuit' — EV installations are specialised work requiring specific kit + certs
Skipping the load test — most homes do not have spare capacity for a 7kW charger without load management
Forgetting weatherproofing + IP rating premium for outdoor installations
Including extra cable runs as 'site survey will tell' — measure first, quote firm

jobs.name: Full Domestic Rewire
jobs.audience: self-employed electricians and small contractors pricing rewires
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (all chapters), Part P notification, Construction (Design and Management) Regulations 2015
keyConsiderations: Price per circuit + per outlet, not per day — a 3-bed has 8-12 circuits and 35-50 outlets typically
Chase + plaster patching is normally excluded — call this out explicitly in the quote
Smoke alarm system to BS 5839-6 Grade D2 is now mandatory on rewires — add the alarm spec separately
AFDDs in HMOs / certain residential settings under A4:2026 — factor in £15-25 per AFDD-protected way
Time on site for a 3-bed rewire is typically 8-15 working days — pad the schedule for snags
Customer-supplied materials (sockets, switches) reduce your margin — quote as 'electrician supplied' by default
commonMistakes: Quoting per day — clients will haggle the day rate and squeeze margin
Including plaster patching in the price (unless you're sub-contracting a plasterer)
Forgetting the EIC + smoke alarm cert + Part P notification
Not factoring in DNO coordination for tails upgrades

jobs.name: Solar PV Installation
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Section 712 (Prosumer's installations), MCS 005, EREC G98/G99 for grid connection, Building Regs Part P
keyConsiderations: MCS certification is the price floor — without it, the homeowner cannot claim SEG payments
Roof-survey time before quote is critical — never quote off photos alone
G98 (≤3.68kW per phase) is notification only; G99 requires DNO pre-approval and adds 4-8 weeks
Battery storage adds 1.5-2x to the system price and falls under Section 712 prosumer rules
Inverter + cable losses + shading need a competent design — that's where MCS-certified earns the margin
commonMistakes: Pricing on raw watts without factoring in roof complexity, scaffold and shading
Quoting battery storage as 'add it later' — Section 712 design should consider battery from day one
Skipping the building-regulations notification — solar is notifiable Part P work in most cases

jobs.name: Emergency Callout (24/7)
jobs.typicalPrice: 120–180 callout fee + £75–110/hour or part hour, plus materials at retail+
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (relevant chapters depending on fault), Electricity at Work Regulations 1989
keyConsiderations: Out-of-hours rates should be 1.5-2x your standard day rate — no exceptions
Bank holiday rates 2-2.5x — your time is worth more, charge accordingly
Charge a callout fee that covers your first hour minimum — even if the fix is 10 minutes
Materials at retail + premium (typically +25-40%) — you're not Screwfix, you're a 24/7 emergency electrician
Make-safe vs full fix — be explicit. Many emergency jobs are make-safe at night, full fix in daylight
commonMistakes: Charging your standard day rate at 11pm on a Saturday — you're underselling your time
Not having a clear callout fee structure — clients will haggle in the moment
Trying to do a full repair at night when a make-safe + return visit is safer and more profitable
Forgetting to charge mileage on rural callouts

jobs.name: Commercial Electrical Installation
jobs.audience: electrical contractors pricing commercial fit-out work
jobs.typicalPrice: Highly variable — £40-70/m² for fit-out, £75-115/hour for project labour
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (all chapters incl. Section 528, 537), BS EN 61439 (assemblies), CDM 2015, Building Regs Part L (energy efficiency)
keyConsiderations: Always insist on a clear scope document with drawings — verbal scope creep eats commercial margins
Three-phase supplies, large distribution boards, fire-rated cabling and SWA add significant material cost
Part L (energy efficiency) increasingly affects lighting design — LED + controls expected
BIM coordination on larger jobs — if the customer requires BIM model, charge for the time
Variations / change orders should be priced before work proceeds — never absorb them
Retention (5-10% held for 12 months) is normal — factor into cashflow
commonMistakes: Pricing commercial at domestic hourly rates — completely different liability + indemnity tier
Not having a clear variation procedure — clients will pile on extras and pay later
Forgetting parking, mileage and welfare on long commercial jobs
Skipping the as-built drawings + O&M pack — these are usually contractually required

jobs.name: PAT Testing (Portable Appliance Testing)
jobs.audience: electricians offering PAT testing as a stand-alone service or bundled with EICR
jobs.typicalPrice: £0.85–£1.50 per item with minimum visit fees of £85–150
jobs.regulatoryRefs: IET Code of Practice for In-Service Inspection and Testing of Electrical Equipment (5th Edition), Electricity at Work Regulations 1989, HSE INDG236
keyConsiderations: Volume drives the rate — 100+ items = £0.85-£1.20 each, 20 items = £1.50-£2.50 each plus visit fee
Minimum visit fee must cover your first hour + travel — never accept jobs at the per-item rate alone
Pass/fail labels + register are part of the deliverable, not extras
Bundle PAT with EICR for commercial premises — efficient on-site and easier to upsell
City & Guilds 2377 (PAT testing course) is the recognised competence qualification
commonMistakes: Per-item only, no minimum visit fee — you'll lose money on small jobs
Missing the IT register update — many corporate clients require their asset register updated
Forgetting to test high-risk equipment (extension leads, multi-way sockets) which are typically 30-50% of failures
Not labelling items clearly — pass-only labels with date is the minimum

jobs.name: Minor Electrical Work (Minor Works Certificate)
jobs.audience: self-employed electricians pricing small notifiable / non-notifiable jobs
jobs.typicalPrice: £95–180 per job (single circuit alteration, new socket on existing circuit)
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 644 + Form 4), Part P (notifiable vs non-notifiable work)
keyConsiderations: Every minor works requires testing (R1+R2, Zs, insulation resistance) and a Minor Works Certificate — bake into the price
Travel + setup time on a 30-minute job is the same as a 4-hour job — minimum visit fee covers it
Materials at retail + (sockets, faceplates, fused spurs)
If the job becomes notifiable mid-way (added a circuit, extended a circuit), it's no longer minor works — quote needs to flex to EIC
Same-day or next-day availability is worth premium pricing
commonMistakes: Skipping the MWC certificate to save time — the certificate IS the deliverable, you're not paid otherwise
Charging £40 for an 'easy' job — your kit, calibration and insurance cost more than that to maintain
Forgetting that adding a circuit is notifiable Part P, not minor works

jobs.name: Commercial EICR
jobs.audience: electrical contractors pricing commercial periodic inspections
jobs.typicalPrice: £18–28 per socket / fitting with minimum visit £380–650
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Chapter 65, IET GN3 9th Edition, Electricity at Work Regulations 1989, RR(FS)O 2005
keyConsiderations: Price per socket / fitting + minimum visit, not per hour — that's the industry-standard pricing model
Three-phase installations and distribution boards take more time per accessory — price band accordingly
Out-of-hours inspection is often required for retail / hospitality (after closing) — premium rates apply
Cleanroom, food-prep, medical premises = specialist work, premium pricing
Report write-up time on a 200-socket commercial premises is significant — bake into pricing
commonMistakes: Pricing commercial EICR at domestic hourly rates — completely different time and liability profile
Not factoring in safe-isolation time on a fully-occupied building
Forgetting the digital register + asset list deliverable expected by most commercial clients

jobs.name: Kitchen Rewire
jobs.typicalPrice: £1,400–2,800 (typical kitchen refurb: sockets, hob, oven, dishwasher, lighting, extractor)
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (cooker circuit, ring vs radial, RCD protection), Building Regs Part P
keyConsiderations: Cooker circuit is the biggest cost driver — 32A or 40A radial, depending on hob/oven combined rating
Under-counter sockets need IPX4 in zones near sinks under Section 528 / kitchen-specific guidance
Dishwasher / washing machine spurs from a ring or radial — confirm with the load calc
LED downlighter installations need fire hoods if in a ceiling void — add to material cost
Coordinate with the kitchen fitter on first-fix vs second-fix sequencing
commonMistakes: Quoting before final kitchen layout is confirmed — appliance positions change everything
Including extractor wiring as 'standard' — many extractors need dedicated isolation point
Forgetting that an island socket installation requires safety considerations not relevant in a standard kitchen

jobs.name: Day-Rate / Sub-Contract Work
jobs.audience: self-employed electricians taking sub-contract day-rate work
jobs.regulatoryRefs: CIS (Construction Industry Scheme) tax rules, IR35 if applicable, JIB pay scales as a benchmark
keyConsiderations: Day rates assume an 8-hour day — anything over should be at time-and-a-half
Travel time is usually NOT paid on day rates — factor in mileage / vehicle wear instead
CIS (Construction Industry Scheme) deductions apply — 20% deducted at source for registered contractors, 30% for unregistered
Self-employed day rates should be 1.5-2x equivalent JIB hourly to cover lack of holiday / pension / sick pay
Always invoice via written contract — verbal day rates lead to disputes
commonMistakes: Accepting JIB-equivalent day rates as self-employed — you carry all the overhead
Not registering for CIS — you'll be deducted 30% at source instead of 20%
Forgetting parking, mileage and welfare on long projects — these eat day-rate margin

jobs.name: Out-of-Hours / Unsocial Work
jobs.audience: electricians offering evening / weekend / night work
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, EAWR 1989 (especially for occupied premises), CDM 2015 if applicable
keyConsiderations: Premium rates are non-negotiable for evenings / weekends / bank holidays — your family time has a price
Retail / hospitality OOH work is typical (work happens after closing) — bill at a clear premium
Safe isolation in occupied premises is harder — add time + supervision cost
Sleep-out / on-call retainers are a separate fee on top of any callout
commonMistakes: Quoting standard rates because 'it's only a couple of hours' — the precedent costs you forever
Not having a clear OOH rate schedule (evenings, weekends, bank holidays) communicated upfront

jobs.name: Maintenance Contract (Annual / Quarterly)
jobs.audience: electrical contractors selling maintenance contracts to commercial / industrial clients
jobs.hourlyTarget: (contract-based, but daily rate during reactive callouts)
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, EAWR 1989, RR(FS)O 2005, ISO 9001 if applicable
keyConsiderations: Maintenance contracts trade margin for predictable cashflow — price for both reactive AND scheduled work
Define response SLAs clearly (4-hour, 24-hour, next business day) and price each tier
Include scheduled EICR / fire alarm / emergency lighting tests where appropriate
Parts at retail+ (typically 25-40% markup on commercial parts)
Annual review + price adjustment for inflation built into the contract
commonMistakes: Pricing the contract at standard hourly rate × estimated hours — leaves no margin for surprise callouts
Including 'unlimited' callouts at the contract price — leaves you exposed to runaway labour
Not annual-reviewing or inflation-adjusting — multi-year contracts erode margin

jobs.name: Smart Home Installation
jobs.audience: electricians pricing smart home retrofits and new-build smart fit-outs
jobs.typicalPrice: £2,500–6,500 (single-room smart upgrade through whole-house Cat6 + PoE lighting)
jobs.regulatoryRefs: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Sections 528, 715), BS EN 50173 (Generic cabling), BS EN 50174 (Cable installation)
keyConsiderations: Smart-home is a premium service — clients pay for design + commissioning, not just install
Whole-house Cat6 + PoE LED is the high-value sweet spot — bundle the package
DALI / KNX certification adds margin — many electricians can install but few can commission
Section 715 ELV crossover with Section 528 segregation must be respected — pricing should reflect the design time
Customer-supplied IoT devices (Ring, Hue, Nest) reduce your margin — quote 'Elec-Mate supplied' as default
commonMistakes: Quoting smart home at standard domestic install rates — completely different design + commissioning effort
Not factoring in the data certification (BS EN 50346 channel test) on the Cat6 / Cat6a installation
Forgetting commissioning time on DALI / KNX — installation is half the job, commissioning is the other half
